summary: The timpani (kettledrums) is the most important percussion instrument of the Classical-era orchestra. Large copper-bodied drums tuned by a foot pedal, developed from the Arabic naker. Most clued as the subject of iconic solos: the opening of Beethoven's Violin Concerto (four soft D's), the opening of Grieg's Piano Concerto (crescendo roll), and Haydn's Symphony No. 103 ('Drumroll') opening solo. Carl Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' Symphony features a famous timpani duel.

works.name: The Timpani Duel in Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' Symphony
works.description: Carl Nielsen's Symphony No. 4 (Det Uudslukkelige, 'The Inextinguishable') features two sets of timpani placed on opposite sides of the orchestra, which 'duel' by playing tritones in the finale before a 'glorioso' coda. The timpani are told to play with 'a certain menacing character' throughout. Nielsen wrote that the symphony depicts the 'elemental will to live.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' Symphony No. 4: two sets of timpani duel in the finale, placed on opposite sides of the orchestra
examples: Two players of these instruments 'duel' in the finale of Carl Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' Symphony

works.name: Beethoven's Violin Concerto – Timpani Opening
works.description: Beethoven's Violin Concerto in D major (Op. 61) opens with five soft D's played by the timpani (sometimes described as 'four D's' or 'four solo D's'), followed by the rest of the orchestra. This is one of the most famous timpani solos in the repertoire. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Opens with four (or five) soft D's played by timpani solo before the rest of the orchestra enters
examples: Four solo D's on this instrument open Beethoven's violin concerto
Solos for this instrument open Beethoven's Violin Concerto and Grieg's Piano Concerto
This instrument has a solo at the beginning of Beethoven's Violin Concerto

works.name: Haydn's Symphony No. 103 – 'Drumroll' Opening
works.description: Haydn's Symphony No. 103 in E-flat major is nicknamed the 'Drumroll' for its opening: a long roll (E-flat) on the timpani that immediately precedes the main theme. After the roll, the lower strings play a slow 3/4-time rendition of the Dies Irae theme. The second movement features a violin solo written for the virtuoso Giovanni Battista Viotti. Johann Christian Fischer wrote an unusual symphony for eight obbligato timpani. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Haydn's Symphony No. 103 nicknamed 'Drumroll' from its opening timpani roll
examples: Haydn's 103rd Symphony earns its nickname from the fact that the introduction features a long 'roll' for this instrument
An opening solo for this instrument inspired the nickname of Haydn's 103rd symphony

clues.clue: After the roll, lower strings play a slow 3/4-time rendition of the Dies Irae
examples: After a short solo from one of these instruments, the lower strings play a slow 3/4-time rendition of the Dies Irae theme at the beginning of an E-flat-major symphony

works.name: Haydn's Mass in Time of War – Paukenmesse
works.description: Haydn's Mass in B-flat major, Hob. XXII:9 (1796), nicknamed the 'Paukenmesse' (German for 'Kettledrum Mass') because of an ominous, foreboding timpani solo in the Agnus Dei. The solo introduces a C-minor section that follows a Benedictus reflecting the 'Time of War.' Wikipedia

clues.clue: Haydn's Mass in Time of War nicknamed 'Paukenmesse' for the ominous timpani solo in the Agnus Dei
examples: The solo for this instrument in the 'Agnus Dei' of the Mass in Time of War lends the mass an alternate nickname
Haydn's Mass in Time of War is alternately known as the 'Paukenmesse' due to its ominous solo for this instrument in the Agnus Dei

works.name: Haydn's 'Surprise' Symphony – Mid-Performance Retuning
works.description: Haydn's Symphony No. 94 in G major ('Surprise') features a notable timpani episode in its first movement: the timpanist is instructed to retune the G-drum up to A (in the first movement, three measures provided for retuning). The 'Surprise' Symphony is the earliest major work in which the timpani are tuned mid-performance; the German nickname ('Paukenschlag') references the fortissimo chord in the second movement. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Surprise Symphony (No. 94): earliest major work with mid-performance retuning; timpanist instructed to tune G-drum up to A in the first movement
examples: The 'Surprise' Symphony is the earliest major work in which these instruments are tuned mid-piece
At one point in this work, the timpanist is instructed to tune his G-drum up to A

clues.clue: German nickname ('Paukenschlag') references the fortissimo chord with timpani in the second movement
examples: its use in producing the fortissimo chord in the 'Surprise' symphony lends that work its German nickname

works.name: General / Instrument Description
works.description: The timpani (kettledrums, Italian pl. from singular timpano) consist of copper hemispherical shells with a membrane stretched over them, tuned using a sliding foot pedal (the Dresden pedal system was developed in the late 1800s, enabling mid-performance retuning). In the Classical era, timpani were primarily used to double trumpets on tonic and dominant notes. Berlioz's Requiem calls for 16 timpani. Carl Nielsen's 'Inextinguishable' features the famous duel. Other notable uses: opens Grieg's Piano Concerto (crescendo roll); four D's open Beethoven's Violin Concerto; opens Sibelius's Symphony No. 1 under a clarinet solo and introduces the scherzo theme; plays triplets on C and G in Also sprach Zarathustra; Prokofiev's Romeo and Juliet uses 15 straight notes for Tybalt's death. Beethoven's Eighth Symphony requires them tuned to octave F's. Developed from the Arabic naker.

clues.clue: Copper-bodied drums tuned using a sliding foot pedal (Dresden pedal system, late 1800s); developed from Arabic naker

comprehensive_summary:
The timpani (kettledrums) is the most commonly clued percussion instrument in quizbowl. These large, copper-bodied drums are tuned using a sliding foot pedal; the Dresden pedal system developed in the late 1800s enabled rapid mid-performance retuning. In the Classical era, timpani were primarily used to double trumpets on tonic and dominant pitches, and were the only percussion instrument in Beethoven's first eight symphonies. They are also used to represent thunder (Berlioz's Symphonie fantastique), hunters' gunshots (Prokofiev's Peter and the Wolf), and various dramatic effects. Berlioz's Requiem unusually calls for 16 timpani. The instrument descended from the Arabic naker.

The single most frequently clued timpani passage is the duel in the finale of Carl Nielsen's Symphony No. 4 ('The Inextinguishable'): two sets of timpani placed on opposite sides of the orchestra play tritones (F/B and D-flat/G) against each other before a 'glorioso' coda, with a 'certain menacing character' throughout. Nielsen wrote the symphony to depict the 'elemental will to live.'

Other iconic timpani solos: Beethoven's Violin Concerto in D major opens with four or five soft D's on solo timpani before the orchestra enters — one of the most famous timpani moments in the repertoire. Grieg's Piano Concerto in A minor opens with a crescendo roll on timpani before the soloist's entry. Haydn's Symphony No. 103 is nicknamed the 'Drumroll' for its opening timpani roll, after which the lower strings play a slow 3/4-time rendition of the Dies Irae. Haydn's Mass in Time of War is nicknamed the 'Paukenmesse' for its ominous Agnus Dei timpani solo. Haydn's 'Surprise' Symphony (No. 94) is the earliest major work in which the timpani are retuned mid-performance (German nickname 'Paukenschlag' references the fortissimo chord in the second movement). In Sibelius's Symphony No. 1, the timpani plays under the opening clarinet solo and later introduces the scherzo theme. In Also sprach Zarathustra (Strauss), the timpani responds to the trumpet fanfare with loud triplets on C and G. Beethoven's Symphony No. 9 scherzo has the opening motif answered by a brief one-measure timpani solo while the orchestra is silent.

Notable speciality works: Elliott Carter's Eight Pieces for Four Timpani; Philip Glass's Concerto Fantasy for Two Timpanists; Johann Christian Fischer's symphony for eight obbligato timpani; Leonard Bernstein called for timpani played with maracas. In Prokofiev's Romeo and Juliet, 15 straight timpani notes signify the death of Tybalt.
